metadata.summary: Multi-language backend standards: Go (project layout, error wrapping, consumer-side interfaces, concurrency), Rails, Laravel, Spring Boot, .NET, and Rust (Result types, async with tokio).

content.title: Backend Languages Practices
content.summary: Reference patterns for six backend languages: Go (cmd/internal/pkg layout, wrapped errors, consumer-side interfaces, worker-pool concurrency), Ruby on Rails (service/form/query objects), PHP Laravel (service pattern), Java Spring Boot (constructor injection), C# .NET (async repository pattern), Rust (thiserror Result types, tokio async). One canonical snippet per pattern.

why.p: A breadth-first reference across languages is how agents handle cross-language onboarding — producing a Django service that mirrors a Spring service, or scaffolding a Go service that matches an existing Rust service's shape. Without a shared reference, agents extrapolate from their training distribution and produce subtly wrong patterns: Go interfaces in the producer package, Spring field injection, Rust blocking inside tokio. This methodology is the cheat sheet the agent loads when the file's language is detected.

list.item: Greenfield service scaffolding in any of the six languages — use as layout template.
Cross-language onboarding: agent must produce equivalent shape in two stacks.
Refactor passes aligning an existing module to the known-good shape.
Code review gate — check Go interfaces are consumer-side, Spring uses constructor injection.
Architecture decisions (microservices vs monolith) — see dev-methodologies-architecture.
Performance profiling or observability — out of scope.
When the project already has a documented house style that diverges — agent will overwrite.

section.name: go-patterns

rule.statement: Go projects MUST use the `cmd/` + `internal/` + `pkg/` layout. Error handling MUST use `fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)` wrapping — never discard errors or return bare strings. Interfaces MUST be defined in the consumer package, not the producer package. Concurrency MUST use the worker-pool pattern with buffered channels.
rule.rationale: The `cmd/internal/pkg` layout is the de facto Go community standard. Error wrapping with `%w` enables `errors.Is/As` unwrapping. Consumer-side interfaces ("accept interfaces, return structs") follow Go's implicit interface satisfaction model and prevent import cycles. Worker pools prevent goroutine leaks.

example.title: Go project layout
example.code: project/ ├── cmd/ │ └── server/ │ └── main.go ├── internal/ │ ├── handlers/ │ ├── services/ │ └── models/ ├── pkg/ # Reusable packages ├── go.mod └── go.sum

example.title: Go error wrapping
Go consumer-side interface
Go worker pool pattern

antipattern.description: Interface defined in the producer package: `package userservice; type UserRepository interface {...}`.
antipattern.reason: Violates Go's "accept interfaces, return structs" idiom. Causes import cycles and forces the producer to anticipate all consumers.

section.note: The README snippet places the interface in the producer package — this is incorrect. Consumer-side is the Go idiom. Correct the agent if it replicates the wrong placement.

rule.statement: Rails projects MUST follow thin-controller architecture: controllers handle only HTTP; business logic goes in service objects; complex validations go in form objects; complex queries go in query objects; view logic goes in presenters.
rule.rationale: Fat models and fat controllers in Rails are the canonical anti-pattern. Service objects, form objects, and query objects keep each class single-responsibility and testable in isolation without the HTTP stack or ActiveRecord.

example.title: Rails architecture objects
example.code: - Thin Controllers → Only HTTP handling - Service Objects → Business logic - Form Objects → Complex validations - Query Objects → Complex queries - Presenters → View logic

rule.statement: Laravel controllers MUST delegate to a service class injected via the constructor. Service classes contain business logic and return Eloquent models. Controllers use Form Requests for validation. Never put business logic directly in a controller method.
rule.rationale: The same rationale as Rails thin-controllers: business logic in controllers is untestable without HTTP. Laravel's service container makes DI easy; there is no excuse for fat controllers.

example.title: Laravel service pattern

rule.statement: Spring Boot services MUST use constructor injection (not field injection via `@Autowired` on fields). Service classes are annotated `@Service @RequiredArgsConstructor`. Repository interfaces extend `JpaRepository`. DTOs use the builder pattern.
rule.rationale: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and enables instantiation in unit tests without a Spring context. `@Autowired` field injection is untestable without the full context. `@RequiredArgsConstructor` from Lombok generates the constructor automatically from `final` fields.

example.title: Spring Boot service with constructor injection

antipattern.description: `@Autowired` on a field: `@Autowired private UserRepository userRepository;`.
antipattern.reason: Field injection requires Spring context in tests; constructor injection lets tests pass mocks directly.

rule.statement: C# .NET services MUST implement an interface (`IUserService`), use constructor injection via the interface, and use `async Task<T>` for all I/O-bound operations. Never use synchronous I/O methods inside an `async` method.
rule.rationale: Interface-based DI enables mock injection in tests. `async/await` throughout the call chain prevents thread starvation under load. Mixing sync and async I/O causes deadlocks in ASP.NET contexts.

example.title: C# async service pattern

rule.statement: Rust projects MUST use `thiserror` for error types, returning `Result<T, E>` from all fallible functions — never `panic!` or `.unwrap()` in library code. Async code MUST use `tokio` and the `#[tokio::main]` entrypoint. Blocking I/O MUST NOT be called inside an async function; use `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` instead.
rule.rationale: Explicit `Result` types make error propagation visible in function signatures. `thiserror` generates `Display` and `From` impls automatically. `reqwest::blocking::Client` inside `#[tokio::main]` compiles but blocks the async runtime, causing deadlocks under load — a common agent mistake.

example.title: Rust thiserror error types
Rust async with tokio
Rust project layout
example.code: project/ ├── Cargo.toml ├── src/ │ ├── main.rs │ ├── lib.rs │ └── modules/ │ ├── mod.rs │ └── user.rs ├── tests/ │ └── integration_test.rs └── examples/

antipattern.description: Using `reqwest::blocking::Client` inside `#[tokio::main]` or any async function.
antipattern.reason: Compiles and runs, but blocks the async runtime thread, causing deadlocks under concurrent load. Use `reqwest::Client` (async) instead.

list.item: For Go: declare interfaces in the consumer package, not the producer. The README snippet contradicts this — correct the agent.
Spring `@Autowired` field injection is shown in many tutorials but discouraged; agents replicate field injection. Force constructor injection in the prompt.
Rust async section uses `reqwest`; agent imports `reqwest::blocking::Client` inside `#[tokio::main]` — compiles, but blocks the runtime. Catch in review.
Mass-rename refactors need a typecheck + test gate per file batch; otherwise the agent commits a half-migrated tree.
For multi-stack work, dispatch one subagent per language so each keeps a focused context.

checklist.check: Go errors use fmt.Errorf wrapping with %w.
Go interfaces defined in consumer package.
Rails controllers are thin; logic in service/form/query objects.
Spring Boot uses constructor injection, not field @Autowired.
Rust uses thiserror Result types, no .unwrap() in library code.
Rust async uses reqwest::Client (async), not blocking variant.
